1→import { defineCollection, reference, z } from "astro:content";
     2→import { glob, type Loader } from "astro/loaders";
     3→
     4→// ============================================================
     5→// Shared schemas (reusable across collections)
     6→// ============================================================
     7→
     8→const seoSchema = z.object({
     9→  metaTitle: z.string().optional(),
    10→  metaDescription: z.string().optional(),
    11→  ogImage: z.string().optional(),
    12→});
    13→
    14→/**
    15→ * A single metric shown on project cards (e.g. "55%↑", "3.2x").
    16→ * Used in project frontmatter and rendered on the homepage showcase.
    17→ */
    18→const metricSchema = z.object({
    19→  value: z.string(),
    20→  label: z.string(),
    21→});
    22→
    23→// ============================================================
    24→// Loader
    25→// ============================================================
    26→const contentLoader = ({ base, extensions }: { extensions: string[]; base: string }): Loader => {
    27→  // id generation: strip file extensions for clean URLs
    28→  const stripExtension = ({ entry }: { entry: string }) => {
    29→    return entry.replace(/\.(mdx?|yaml)$/, "");
    30→  };
    31→
    32→  // filter drafts only in production
    33→  const draftFilter = import.meta.env.MODE === "development" ? "**" : "!(*.draft)";
    34→  // filter extensions
    35→  const extensionFilter = extensions.length > 1 ? `{${extensions.join(",")}}` : extensions[0];
    36→
    37→  return glob({
    38→    pattern: `**/${draftFilter}.${extensionFilter}`,
    39→    base,
    40→    generateId: stripExtension,
    41→  });
    42→};
    43→
    44→// ============================================================
    45→// Collections
    46→// ============================================================
    47→
    48→const projects = defineCollection({
    49→  loader: contentLoader({
    50→    base: "src/content/projects",
    51→    extensions: ["mdx"],
    52→  }),
    53→  schema: z.object({
    54→    title: z.string(),
    55→    tagline: z.string().optional(),
    56→    client: z.string().optional(),
    57→    date: z.coerce.date(),
    58→    thumbnail: z.string(),
    59→    /** Large hero image for homepage showcase and project detail header */
    60→    heroImage: z.string().optional(),
    61→    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    62→    /** Industry categories for filtering (e.g., "fintech", "e-commerce", "healthcare") */
    63→    industry: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    64→    /** Deliverable types for filtering (e.g., "website", "mobile-app", "design-system") */
    65→    deliverable: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    66→    /** Key metrics shown on project cards (e.g. conversion uplift, NPS) */
    67→    metrics: z.array(metricSchema).default([]),
    68→    seo: seoSchema.optional(),
    69→    relatedProjects: z.array(reference("projects")).default([]),
    70→  }),
    71→});
    72→
    73→const blog = defineCollection({
    74→  loader: contentLoader({
    75→    extensions: ["md", "mdx"],
    76→    base: "src/content/blog",
    77→  }),
    78→  schema: z.object({
    79→    title: z.string(),
    80→    description: z.string().optional(),
    81→    date: z.coerce.date(),
    82→    author: z.string().optional(),
    83→    thumbnail: z.string().optional(),
    84→    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    85→    seo: seoSchema.optional(),
    86→    relatedProjects: z.array(reference("projects")).default([]),
    87→  }),
    88→});
    89→
    90→const playground = defineCollection({
    91→  loader: contentLoader({
    92→    extensions: ["md", "mdx"],
    93→    base: "src/content/playground",
    94→  }),
    95→  schema: z.object({
    96→    title: z.string(),
    97→    description: z.string(),
    98→    date: z.coerce.date(),
    99→    repoUrl: z.string().url().optional(),
   100→    thumbnail: z.string().optional(),
   101→    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
   102→    seo: seoSchema.optional(),
   103→  }),
   104→});
   105→
   106→const pages = defineCollection({
   107→  loader: contentLoader({
   108→    extensions: ["mdx"],
   109→    base: "src/content/pages",
   110→  }),
   111→  schema: z.object({
   112→    title: z.string(),
   113→    seo: seoSchema.optional(),
   114→  }),
   115→});
   116→
   117→// ============================================================
   118→// Homepage highlights
   119→// ============================================================
   120→// Each entry references an item in another collection.
   121→// Astro validates every slug at build time: typos break the build, not prod.
   122→
   123→const highlights = defineCollection({
   124→  loader: contentLoader({
   125→    extensions: ["yaml"],
   126→    base: "src/content/highlights",
   127→  }),
   128→  schema: z.object({
   129→    label: z.string().optional(),
   130→    source: z.discriminatedUnion("collection", [
   131→      z.object({
   132→        collection: z.literal("projects"),
   133→        entry: reference("projects"),
   134→      }),
   135→      z.object({
   136→        collection: z.literal("blog"),
   137→        entry: reference("blog"),
   138→      }),
   139→      z.object({
   140→        collection: z.literal("playground"),
   141→        entry: reference("playground"),
   142→      }),
   143→    ]),
   144→    order: z.number(),
   145→  }),
   146→});
   147→
   148→// ============================================================
   149→// Clients — logo strip, references across the site
   150→// ============================================================
   151→// YAML entries for each client. Used by the homepage logo strip,
   152→// project pages, and anywhere we need to show who we work with.
   153→
   154→const clients = defineCollection({
   155→  loader: contentLoader({
   156→    extensions: ["yaml"],
   157→    base: "src/content/clients",
   158→  }),
   159→  schema: z.object({
   160→    name: z.string(),
   161→    /** Path to SVG logo in public/ (e.g. "/logos/boeing.svg") */
   162→    logo: z.string(),
   163→    url: z.string().url().optional(),
   164→    order: z.number().default(0),
   165→  }),
   166→});
   167→
   168→// ============================================================
   169→// Testimonials — customer quotes
   170→// ============================================================
   171→// YAML entries for client testimonials. Used on the homepage,
   172→// and potentially on project pages or a dedicated social proof section.
   173→
   174→const testimonials = defineCollection({
   175→  loader: contentLoader({
   176→    extensions: ["yaml"],
   177→    base: "src/content/testimonials",
   178→  }),
   179→  schema: z.object({
   180→    quote: z.string(),
   181→    author: z.string(),
   182→    role: z.string(),
   183→    company: z.string(),
   184→    /** CDN path to author avatar (optional) */
   185→    avatar: z.string().optional(),
   186→    order: z.number().default(0),
   187→  }),
   188→});
   189→
   190→// ============================================================
   191→// Awards — recognition and validation
   192→// ============================================================
   193→// YAML entries for awards and recognitions. Shown on the homepage
   194→// in a scrollable list. Each award references the project it was for.
   195→
   196→const awards = defineCollection({
   197→  loader: contentLoader({
   198→    extensions: ["yaml"],
   199→    base: "src/content/awards",
   200→  }),
   201→  schema: z.object({
   202→    /** Award name (e.g. "Red Dot", "Awwwards Site of the Day") */
   203→    award: z.string(),
   204→    year: z.number(),
   205→    /** Project that won the award — validated at build time */
   206→    project: reference("projects"),
   207→    /** External link to the award page (optional) */
   208→    url: z.string().url().optional(),
   209→    order: z.number().default(0),
   210→  }),
   211→});
   212→
   213→// ============================================================
   214→// Handbook groups — visual categories on the handbook index
   215→// ============================================================
   216→// Each group has a title and an order. Handbook pages reference
   217→// a group by its filename slug (e.g. "engineering", "design").
   218→
   219→const handbookGroups = defineCollection({
   220→  loader: contentLoader({
   221→    extensions: ["yaml"],
   222→    base: "src/content/handbook-groups",
   223→  }),
   224→  schema: z.object({
   225→    title: z.string(),
   226→    order: z.number(),
   227→  }),
   228→});
   229→
   230→// ============================================================
   231→// Handbook — internal company documentation
   232→// ============================================================
   233→// MDX files in src/content/handbook/, organized in directories
   234→// for parent-child nesting. File path determines URL:
   235→//   culture.mdx                      → /handbook/culture
   236→//   how-we-build-software.mdx        → /handbook/how-we-build-software
   237→//   how-we-build-software/front-end.mdx → /handbook/how-we-build-software/front-end
   238→//
   239→// `group` references a handbook-groups entry (validated at build time).
   240→// `order` controls sort position within a group on the index page.
   241→// Parent pages (those with children) are identified at query time
   242→// by checking if any other entry's id starts with their id + "/".
   243→
   244→const handbook = defineCollection({
   245→  loader: contentLoader({
   246→    extensions: ["mdx"],
   247→    base: "src/content/handbook",
   248→  }),
   249→  schema: z.object({
   250→    title: z.string(),
   251→    description: z.string().optional(),
   252→    /** Last updated date */
   253→    date: z.coerce.date(),
   254→    /** Cover image displayed at the top of the page (CDN path) */
   255→    coverImage: z.string().optional(),
   256→    /** Reference to a handbook-groups entry */
   257→    group: reference("handbook-groups"),
   258→    /** Sort order within the group on the index page */
   259→    order: z.number().default(0),
   260→    seo: seoSchema.optional(),
   261→  }),
   262→});
   263→
   264→export const collections = {
   265→  projects,
   266→  blog,
   267→  playground,
   268→  pages,
   269→  highlights,
   270→  clients,
   271→  testimonials,
   272→  awards,
   273→  "handbook-groups": handbookGroups,
   274→  handbook,
   275→};

     2→title: Designing complex products through client collaboration
     3→description: You don't need to become an industry expert to design a great product, but you do need to collaborate effectively, ask the right questions, simplify complexity when you can, and validate your assumptions with real users.
     4→date: 2026-01-22
     5→author: Teresa Araújo
     6→thumbnail: https://cdn.significa.co/blog/designing-complex-products/hero.jpg

     8→  - design
     9→  - guide

    11→  - mishmash
    12→seo:
    13→  metaTitle: Designing complex products through client collaboration — Significa
    14→  metaDescription: Learn how to design for unfamiliar industries through structured collaboration, domain modeling, and continuous feedback loops.
    15→---
    16→
    17→I recently designed an insurance management product. My knowledge of insurance? Zero.
    18→
    19→I could barely tell you the difference between premiums and brokers, let alone design a platform that finance professionals would use daily to reconcile thousands of payments. But here's the thing: designers rarely start projects as domain experts, and we don't need to be. What matters is working closely with those who are. The real skill isn't becoming an insurance expert overnight, but knowing how to collaborate effectively so you can translate complex industry logic into something intuitive.

---
title: Designing complex products through client collaboration
description: You don't need to become an industry expert to design a great product, but you do need to collaborate effectively, ask the right questions, simplify complexity when you can, and validate your assumptions with real users.
date: 2026-01-22
author: Teresa Araújo
thumbnail: https://cdn.significa.co/blog/designing-complex-products/hero.jpg
tags:
  - design
  - guide
relatedProjects:
  - mishmash
seo:
  metaTitle: Designing complex products through client collaboration — Significa
  metaDescription: Learn how to design for unfamiliar industries through structured collaboration, domain modeling, and continuous feedback loops.
---

I recently designed an insurance management product. My knowledge of insurance? Zero.

I could barely tell you the difference between premiums and brokers, let alone design a platform that finance professionals would use daily to reconcile thousands of payments. But here's the thing: designers rarely start projects as domain experts, and we don't need to be. What matters is working closely with those who are. The real skill isn't becoming an insurance expert overnight, but knowing how to collaborate effectively so you can translate complex industry logic into something intuitive.

content.text:
Analisei os dois nodes. Conceito excelente. Deixa-me desmontar o que encontrei e propor uma abordagem sistematizada.

---

## O que vi no Figma

**PixelContainer (o mosaico/tile):** Uma célula de 2px x 2px com uma elipse ("ovo") centrada. A elipse tem 4 tamanhos dentro da célula:

| Nível | Largura | Altura | Escala (~) |
|-------|---------|--------|------------|
| xs    | 0.5px   | 0.55px | 25%        |
| s     | 1.0px   | 1.15px | 50%        |
| m     | 1.5px   | 1.75px | 75%        |
| l     | 1.74px  | 2.0px  | 87%        |

Os ovos são ligeiramente mais altos que largos (ratio ~1.15). O tamanho L ancora ao fundo da célula; os outros ficam centrados.

**IconArrow (a composição):** Uma grid 7x7 de PixelContainers (7 colunas x 2px = 14px). Tracei as duas states célula a célula:

```
idle:                    hover:
0 0 2 0 0 0 0           0 0 0 2 0 0 0
0 0 3 3 0 0 0           0 0 0 3 3 0 0
2 3 3 4 4 0 0           1 2 3 3 4 4 0
2 3 3 4 4 4 0           1 2 3 3 4 4 4
2 3 3 4 4 0 0           1 2 3 3 4 4 0
0 0 0 3 3 0 0           0 0 0 0 3 3 0
0 0 0 2 0 0 0           0 0 0 0 2 0 0
```

Onde 0=invisível, 1=xs, 2=s, 3=m, 4=l.

O hover é o idle deslocado 1 coluna para a direita, com um "rasto" de ovos xs (valor 1) na trailing edge. Isto cria a ilusão de a seta se mover. O gradiente de tamanhos (xs→s→m→l) funciona como anti-aliasing sub-pixel. Muito bem pensado.

---

## Sistema proposto

### Modelo de dados

Cada icon é uma grid flat de valores 0-4, com opcionalmente uma segunda grid para hover:

```typescript
// lib/mosaic-icons.ts
export type MosaicLevel = 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4;

export interface MosaicIconDef {
  cols: number;
  idle: MosaicLevel[];
  hover?: MosaicLevel[];  // se omitido, sem transição
}

// Escala de cada nível (fração do cell size)
export const MOSAIC_SCALES = [0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.87] as const;

export const mosaicIcons = {
  arrow: {
    cols: 7,
    idle: [
      0,0,2,0,0,0,0,
      0,0,3,3,0,0,0,
      2,3,3,4,4,0,0,
      2,3,3,4,4,4,0,
      2,3,3,4,4,0,0,
      0,0,0,3,3,0,0,
      0,0,0,2,0,0,0,
    ],
    hover: [
      0,0,0,2,0,0,0,
      0,0,0,3,3,0,0,
      1,2,3,3,4,4,0,
      1,2,3,3,4,4,4,
      1,2,3,3,4,4,0,
      0,0,0,0,3,3,0,
      0,0,0,0,2,0,0,
    ],
  },
  // futuros icons: plus, menu, close, check, etc.
} as const satisfies Record<string, MosaicIconDef>;
```

Definir um novo icon = escrever uma grid de números. O gradiente de tamanhos nas bordas cria o efeito visual. Não precisa de designers a exportar SVGs.

### Renderização (Astro component)

CSS Grid puro. Cada célula é um `<span>` com um pseudo-element `::before` que é o ovo (border-radius 50%, background currentColor). Inline styles com `--i` (idle scale) e `--h` (hover scale) por célula.

A transição hover funciona via CSS custom property herdada do parent:

1. O parent (ex: `.highlight-row`) define `--mosaic-state: 0` (idle)
2. No `:hover`, muda para `--mosaic-state: 1`
3. Cada ovo interpola entre idle e hover: `calc(var(--i) + (var(--h) - var(--i)) * var(--mosaic-state))`
4. A transição está no `width` e `height` do pseudo-element (nativamente animáveis)

Resultado: todos os ovos morfam simultaneamente, cada um com a sua trajetória de escala. Sem JS. Sem `:global()`. A cascade CSS faz o trabalho.

### Por que esta abordagem

- **Zero JS.** Astro component estático, sem hydration, sem React island.
- **currentColor.** Herda a cor do contexto. O highlight-row já muda cor no hover, o icon acompanha automaticamente.
- **Transição orgânica.** Os ovos escalam individualmente, criando um morph suave, não um swap abrupto.
- **Escalável.** Definir novos icons é preencher uma grid de números. O renderer é genérico.
- **Sub-pixel fidelity.** A 2px/cell em displays retina, os ovos a 50%/75% renderizam com detalhe real. Em 1x, o rounding do browser cria o look pixel-art naturalmente.

### Questões para discutir antes de implementar

1. **Ovos circulares vs ovais?** No Figma, os ovos são ~15% mais altos que largos. A 2px cells a diferença é sub-pixel. Recomendo círculos (mais simples), com possibilidade de adicionar `--egg-ratio` se quisermos. Faz sentido?

2. **Cell size fixo em 2px?** Ou queremos suportar outras densidades (ex: icons 28px = 7x7 grid com cell 4px)? Posso tornar o cell size configurável via prop.

3. **Stagger na transição?** Podemos adicionar um `transition-delay` por coluna para criar um efeito de "onda" na transição (o rasto aparece primeiro, a ponta chega depois). Adicionaria ~15ms por coluna. Mais polish, mais complexidade. Vale a pena no primeiro pass?

4. **Tenho as grids corretas?** Tracei as 49 células do arrow a partir do código Figma MCP. Vale conferir rapidamente no Figma se o padrão visual bate.

Diz-me o que pensas e avanço para implementação.

Here's what I found: ### 1. **Config File Location** - **Correct file:** `/Users/andrefurt/code/significa.co/src/content.config.ts` (the `src/content/config.ts` location doesn't exist) - Uses Astro v5's new loader-based system with `glob()` and `Loader` pattern ### 2. **Highlights Collection Definition** (lines 118-146 in content.config.ts) The highlights collection is fully defined: ```typescript const highlights = defineCollection({ loader: contentLoader({ extensions: ["yaml"], base: "src/content/highlights", }), schema: z.object({ label: z.string().optional(), source: z.discriminatedUnion("collection", [ z.object({ collection: z.literal("projects"), entry: reference("projects"), }), z.object({ collection: z.literal("blog"), entry: reference("blog"), }), z.object({ collection: z.literal("playground"), entry: reference("playground"), }), ]), order: z.number(), }), }); ``` **Key features:** - Uses discriminated unions to allow referencing projects, blog posts, or playground entries - `reference()` provides build-time validation (typos in slugs break the build) - `order` field controls sort position - `label` is optional (e.g., "Featured Project", "From the Blog") ### 3. **Highlights Directory Contents** Location: `/Users/andrefurt/code/significa.co/src/content/highlights/` **Files:** - `01-example-project.yaml` - references project "mishmash" (order: 1, label: "Featured Project") - `02-planit.yaml` - references project "planit-app" (order: 2, no label) - `03-fintech-blog.yaml` - references blog post "designing-for-fintech" (order: 3, label: "From the Blog") - `04-svelte-ui.yaml` - references playground entry "example-lab" (order: 4, label: "Open Source") ### 4. **Collection Helper Functions** (/Users/andrefurt/code/significa.co/src/lib/collections.ts) **`getResolvedHighlights()` function (lines 48-64):** ```typescript export async function getResolvedHighlights() { const allHighlights = await getCollection("highlights"); const sorted = allHighlights.sort((a, b) => a.data.order - b.data.order); const resolved = await Promise.all( sorted.map(async (h) => { const entry = await getEntry(h.data.source.collection, h.data.source.entry.id); return { label: h.data.label, collection: h.data.source.collection, entry, }; }) ); return resolved; } ``` **What it does:** 1. Fetches all highlights from the collection 2. Sorts them by `order` field (ascending) 3. Resolves each reference to its actual entry (project, blog post, or playground item) 4. Returns an array of objects with `label`, `collection`, and the full `entry` data **Currently used:** Not imported anywhere in the codebase yet (defined but unused) ### 5. **Homepage Integration** (/Users/andrefurt/code/significa.co/src/pages/index.astro) The highlights section uses a static `HighlightCard` component (lines 138-156): ```astro
``` **Current state:** Using hardcoded testimonial-style cards, NOT using the highlights collection data. ### 6. **HighlightCard Component** (/Users/andrefurt/code/significa.co/src/components/highlight-card.astro) Props interface: - `title` (required): card heading - `subtitle` (optional): supporting text - `bg`, `fg`: CSS color values for background/foreground - `size`: "large" | "small" (controls typography) - `href` (optional): if provided, renders as `` tag instead of `
` The component is ready to accept dynamic content from the highlights collection. ### 7. **Architecture Summary** ``` src/content/highlights/*.yaml ↓ [contentLoader with glob()] ↓ highlights collection (defined in content.config.ts) ↓ getResolvedHighlights() helper ↓ Returns: { label, collection, entry }[] ↓ HighlightCard component (ready to accept data) ``` ### Key Insights 1. **The infrastructure exists but isn't connected**: `getResolvedHighlights()` is defined but never called. The homepage hardcodes highlights instead of querying the collection. 2. **Build-time validation is enforced**: The `reference()` function means any broken slug in a `.yaml` file will fail the build—typos are caught immediately, not in production. 3. **Flexible reference system**: A single highlights collection can pull from projects, blog, or playground, each with its own schema validation. 4. **Filename-based ordering**: The `01-`, `02-`, `03-` prefixes in filenames are cosmetic; the `order` field in YAML is what controls actual position. 5. **Ready for implementation**: To use the collection, you just need to call `getResolvedHighlights()` in the homepage and iterate over the resolved entries, passing data to `HighlightCard`.
